heroHeading: Modular Home Extensions, Made for Harrow
metaTitle: Modular Home Extensions in Harrow | EazyBase
metaDescription: Factory-built modular home extensions for Harrow homes — designed around Metroland semis, installed on-site in under a week. Kitchens, offices & more.
intro:
Harrow is a borough of homes people hold on to. From the bay-fronted Metroland semis of North Harrow and Rayners Lane to the family houses of Pinner and Stanmore, these are properties bought for the long haul — for the schools, the gardens, the ten-minute walk to the station. The trouble is, family life has a habit of outgrowing the floorplan before anyone is ready to leave it.

That's where modular home extensions in Harrow come into their own. EazyBase designs your extension around your home, builds it in our Blackburn factory in as little as four weeks, then installs it on-site in under a week. A bigger kitchen, a proper home office, a playroom, a garden room — added with a fraction of the disruption of a traditional build.

No months of scaffolding, no skip on the drive from spring to autumn. Just a precise, factory-finished space that feels like it was always meant to be there.

localAngles.heading: Extending a Metroland semi
localAngles.body: The 1930s semis that define so much of Harrow — Kenton, North Harrow, Rayners Lane, Headstone — were generously built for their time, but their kitchens rarely suit the way families cook and gather now. A single-storey rear extension is the classic answer, opening the back of the house into a kitchen-diner that runs the width of the plot. Because our modules are precision-built in the factory, we design around the quirks of interwar housing stock — bay returns, outriggers, shared side passages — rather than discovering them halfway through a long on-site build.

localAngles.heading: Planning in Harrow
localAngles.body: Much of the borough's suburban housing benefits from permitted development rights, which often cover single-storey rear extensions within set size limits — though you should always check with Harrow Council before committing, as rights vary from house to house. Harrow-on-the-Hill is a different matter: its conservation areas rightly come with tighter controls, and some streets elsewhere carry Article 4 directions or previous alterations that change the picture. We help you understand what applies to your home early on, so the design you fall in love with is one you can actually build.

localAngles.heading: Extending instead of moving
localAngles.body: Plenty of Harrow families find themselves in the same bind: the house is one room too small, but moving means giving up a school catchment, a commute that works and neighbours you actually like — before you've even counted stamp duty and moving costs. An extension lets you stay put and gain the space you'd have moved for. A playroom that clears the lounge of toy boxes, a dedicated office that gives the spare bedroom back, a dining space big enough for both sets of grandparents. Same address, same school run, more house.

localAngles.heading: Why factory-built works in Harrow
localAngles.body: Harrow's residential streets weren't laid out with six months of building work in mind. Traditional extensions mean deliveries, trades and skips on tight suburban roads for a season or more. Ours arrive largely complete. Your extension is manufactured in our Blackburn facility — weatherproof, insulated and finished to a factory standard no rained-off site can match — then installed at your home in under a week. For busy family neighbourhoods, from Pinner's cul-de-sacs to Stanmore's avenues, that difference in disruption is exactly the point.

faqs.q: How long would an extension take for a home in Harrow?
faqs.a: Your extension is built in our Blackburn factory in as little as four weeks while your home carries on as normal. Once it arrives in Harrow, on-site installation typically takes under a week — so the period of actual disruption at your house is measured in days, not months.

faqs.q: Will I need planning permission in Harrow?
faqs.a: Often not. Permitted development rights frequently cover single-storey rear extensions within certain size limits, though every property is different — and homes in Harrow-on-the-Hill's conservation areas face stricter rules. We review your property's situation at the design stage and always recommend confirming with Harrow Council before work begins.

faqs.q: Can you install on a typical Harrow street, with parking and neighbours close by?
faqs.a: Yes — it's what modular installation is designed for. We survey access in advance, plan the delivery and lift around your street's constraints, and keep the on-site phase to days rather than months. Compared with a traditional build, your neighbours will barely notice we were there.

faqs.q: What do Harrow homeowners most often use an extension for?
faqs.a: Open-plan kitchen-diners are the favourite, especially on Metroland semis where the original kitchen feels tight. Home offices, kids' playrooms and garden rooms are close behind — usually chosen by families who'd rather add the room they're missing than leave a house, and a school catchment, they love.
